clean_story: Shared enjoyment.  A signal of connection. "Like" comes from the Old English lician, meaning "to please"—a quick way to say, "This resonates with me. "It’s like nodding at a stranger’s joke across a crowded room—no words, just instant agreement. On screens, it’s faster:A thumb-tap replaces applause, laughter, or even silence. Facebook’s 2009 "Like" button turned approval into data. Each click trains algorithms, shaping what you see next—like teaching a dog tricks with treats. But likes aren’t just numbers. A 2016 study found posts with 50+ likes trigger mirror neurons, making viewers feel the popularity. It’s why a heart on a photo can feel like a hug. Offline, "like" is subtler:Leaning in during a story. Copying a friend’s slang. Even yawns spread faster among people who like each other. Three related subjects are mirror neuron effects, digital body language, and social validation loops.
